data.title: Globe Life And GL.PR.D: Undervalued Investment Class With Growth Potential
data.description:
The following lines are about one of our top picks from the Insurance sector, namely the baby bond (GL.PR.D) of the company Globe Life Inc. (GL).

Company Basic Information

  • Total assets Q3 2025: around $30.5 billion
  • Current market capitalization: around $10.66 billion
  • Credit ratings:

  • Historical performance

    Globe Life's 10-year total return on NAV is approximately 9.2%, while the 5-year figure stands around 9.15%.

  • The Baby Bond

(GL.PR.D) pays an annual interest rate of 4.25% through quarterly distributions of $0.2656, or $1.0625 annually. It was issued on 06/09/2021 with 13 million shares at a par value of $25 each, generating $325 million in gross proceeds. The spread to the 20-year treasury yield at its IPO date was 2.15%, and currently, this spread is 2.14%. The bond is rated Baa2 by Moody's and BBB+ by S&P, and has approximately 0.59 years to call, with a call date of 06/15/2026. Below are shown the basic metrics of the bonds:

GL-D's yield to maturity is close to 6.79%, and the maturity date is on 06/15/2061 ( with close to 35.6 years to maturity). We are facing a lot of questions about yield calculations, and we calculate them using the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is supposed to be the most precise way when dealing with ex-dividend dates. It is a bit more work than just finding a random bond yield calculator, but at least we try to ensure the data is accurate. Below you can see GL-D's yields to worst at different prices:

Comparison with peers

Below is shown how GL.PR.D stands among its peers in the Insurance sector, trading below par and with investment-grade credit ratings.

The OTC bonds of GL are rated Baa1 by Moody's - one notch higher than the baby bonds - but their yield range is between 4.1% and 5.1%. The highest-yielding bond offers a YTM of around 5.1%, which is 1.7% lower than that of GL.PR.D. If the bond were to trade at a similar yield, its price would be approximately $22, representing 17% upside potential.

We have chosen OTC bonds with high duration that have ratings between BAA1 and BAA3 by Moody's:

The spread to our bond is close to 1%, or more than 11% capital appreciation potential if, at some point, the market decides to treat GL's exchange-traded debt similarly to the overall bond market.

Conclusion

Globe Life demonstrates a solid financial position, supported by high credit ratings and a solid long-term performance. GL.PR.D offers an attractive yield relative to similar issues and is trading below par, creating the potential for capital gains as market spreads normalize. Overall, GL.PR.D represents a relatively low-risk investment, suitable for investors seeking eventual growth potential.

data.title: VanEck Mid-November 2025 Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) selloff is being driven by mid-cycle holders, not long-term whales, with futures markets flashing deeply oversold conditions after tariff-driven liquidations.

Please note that VanEck has exposure to bitcoin.

Bitcoin (BTC) Investors are Afraid

ETP Outflows Drive Early Weakness

The last 30 days of price action have been particularly unkind to HOLDers, with BTC down -13% on highly motivated selling. Since October 10, 2025, BTC ETP balances have bled 49.3K BTC, around -2% of total AUM, as the weak hands who bought near the price peak capitulated amid rate cut uncertainty and wobbles in the AI narrative. More concerning, many are pointing the finger at early BTC whales for the price weakness. For example, a “Satoshi Era” Whale sold $1.5B of BTC, his entire wallet, the week of November 14, 2025. Many suppose that tenured whales often telegraph long-term moves in BTC by buying or selling BTC at pivotal junctures. As a result, the crypto community has become bearish, as indicated by the fear/greed index hitting its lowest level since March 2025 at the beginning of the tariff tantrum.

Smaller Whales Accumulate Over 1-2 Years as Largest Whales Distribute; Recent Net Change is Flat

Whales Positions Lower Long-Term, Higher Short Term

100-1K (%) 1K-10K (%) 10K-100K (%) above 100K (%) Total Whale Holdings (%)
30-Day Change -1 0 3 1 0
60-Day Change 2 -3 3 -2 0
90-Day Change 3 -3 1 1 1
180-Day Change 9 1 -6 -2 1
1-Year Change 23 -3 -11 -8 3
2-Year Change 31 -5 -9 -4 6

Source: Glassnode as of 11/13/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Rather than assuming recent weakness stems from large holder selling, it helps to examine the full distribution of flows across cohorts. The onchain picture shows a more nuanced rotation than simple “whale dumping.” If we look at holdings of whales with more than 1,000 BTC, we can see clearly that they have been reducing their BTC exposure since November 2023. In fact, whales with 10K-100K BTC have reduced their supply by -6% and -11% over the last 6 months and 12 months. This supply has been absorbed by “minnows” holding between 100 and 1,000 BTC. This smaller class of investors has increased holdings +9% and +23% over the past 6 months and 12 months, respectively. For context, BTC itself is up ~170% over the last two years.

Bitcoin Futures Open Interest +6% in November

Short-Term Whales Flip to Net Buyers

Short-term data tells a different story: some whale cohorts have been accumulating. The 10K–100K BTC group has increased holdings by about +3%, +2.5%, and +84 bps over the last 30, 60, and 90 days. This likely reflects the tariff-driven selloff and subsequent liquidations, which cut BTC futures open interest by about 19% in 12 hours and pushed the price lower by more than 20%.

Oldest BTC Whales Are Holding While Mid-Cycle Traders Sell

Last Active 6m-2y Last Active 3y-5y Last Active 5y-10y Last Active >10y
30-Day Change -202,674 -272,996 82,500 9,608
180-Day Change 705,516 -592,745 20,079 -22,381
1-Year Change 176,954 -855,050 -113,033 39,374
2-Year Change 204,266 -1,706,293 -92,959 382,379

Source: Glassnode as of 11/13/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Mid-Cycle Holders Are the Real Sellers

However, simply analyzing “whale data” by holder size provides an incomplete picture. This view overlooks the rotation of aged, experienced whales transferring their coins to newer, greenhorn holders. To deepen our understanding, we examine Bitcoin balances by “last actively moved,” which indicates the time elapsed since the token was last transferred. The implication of a transfer is that the tokens were likely sold to different holders.

Over the past 30 days, selling pressure has been concentrated in the <5-year age bands, while older tokens have largely retained or increased their holdings. Interestingly, over the last 6 months, ownership has shifted from the (3–5 yr) group to the (6m–2 yr) cluster, signaling a move from mid-term holders toward newer participants.

Among the older cohort, those whose coins last moved >5 years ago, token turnover remains low relative to other groups. In contrast, the largest churn has occurred among tokens last moved 3–5 yrs ago, a band that has consistently declined across each study period. Over the past two years, supply in this tranche has dropped by 32% as coins were sent to new addresses. Given that many of these tokens were likely accumulated during the doldrums of the previous Bitcoin cycle, their holders appear to be opportunistic cycle traders rather than long-term investors.

Meanwhile, tokens last moved >5 yrs ago have seen a net increase of +278K BTC compared to two years ago. This gain reflects younger coins aging into the 5+ yr category rather than renewed accumulation, yet it still indicates continued conviction among long-term whales. While further granularity could yield additional insights, the overarching trend remains encouraging: the longest-term holders continue to accumulate and hold.

BTC Futures Basis at Lowest Levels Since Fall 2023

Futures Markets Show a Speculation Reset

One of the best measures of speculation is the annualized basis costs paid by traders willing to be long Bitcoin perpetual futures (perps). Because perps never settle, perp prices are brought in line with spot prices by means of an interest rate charged to one counterparty of the trade. If the perp’s price is above the spot price, the long side of the trade must pay the short side an interest rate relative to the magnitude of the spot/perp price difference. Because crypto has asymmetric upside, perp basis is almost always positive.

During periods of lower demand for long crypto like BTC, the basis collapses. Recently, we saw a dramatic collapse in open interest for Bitcoin perps, down -20% in BTC terms since October 9, 2025, and -32% in USD terms. This partly explains the substantial collapse in funding rates. Of course, if people were bullish about BTC, this rate would quickly climb.

In the past, long-term downturns in BTC price were often preceded by blow-off tops of speculation where perp funding averaged 40% on some days. We have not seen that sort of acceleration of funding since March 2024. However, to cloud the picture a bit, it is important to remember that projects like Ethena, as well as sophisticated traders, have accumulated massive basis positions of long spot crypto and short perps. Ethena alone had reached a TVL of $14B in October 2025, and it has since seen its TVL collapse to $8.3B. These large figures of basis trades may artificially depress funding rates, making the indicator no longer effective.

That caveat noted, funding rate collapses of the magnitude we have just witnessed are typically associated with oversold conditions. This is particularly true when we see a concurrent collapse in perps open interest of the ferocity that recently transpired. Additionally, NUPL, or Net Unrealized Profit/Loss ratio, has hit tactically oversold levels that match those seen during the Tariff Tantrum in Spring 2025 and the Yen implosion in August 2024. Armed with this data, investors can proceed more tactically bullish after a month of very dramatic selling, in our opinion.

data.title: AMZY: Harnessing The Volatility Of Amazon For Weekly Income
data.description:
Overview

Now that many of the YieldMax ETFs have operating histories longer than a year, we have a better idea of how the funds will perform through different market environments. When I previously covered the YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (AMZY), I issued a buy rating due to the potential tax efficiency and total return that has stayed on par with the underlying common shares of Amazon (AMZN). I am bullish on AMZN, so I wanted to revisit the fund to reinforce why it can be a great accompanying position for investors that are optimistic on the outlook of the company.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that AMZY's share price has declined by about 30.2%. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 16.9% over the same time frame. AMZY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of approximately 37.4% and issues payouts on a weekly basis. Therefore, AMZY can be an enticing choice for investors seeking a high level of income generation within their portfolio. However, new payouts are declared every week, so the estimated distribution rate can be different by the time you read this article.

While I have become fond of these high-yield option ETFs, I believe there are some major risks that should be considered before initiating a position. Additionally, a fund like AMZY may not be the best fit for conservative income investors that want to focus on sustainability and reliability through market uncertainty. There are also some peer funds that may be a better fit for investors, so I wanted to run a quick comparison to measure the strengths and weaknesses of these alternative choices. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that AMZY implements to generate the necessary income levels to support the high distributions.

Fund Strategy

AMZY has an inception dating back to July of 2023 and has a gross expense ratio of 0.99%. The fund now has total net assets of $297.6M and aims to provide weekly income from its option writing strategy. However, the fund utilizes a synthetic option strategy, which can increase the overall risk and volatility for long-term investors. The term 'synthetic' means that AMZY doesn't actually hold any common shares of AMZN, so it is writing options against an equity that it doesn't own.

As we can see from the holdings below, AMZY is mostly made up of US Treasury bills and different AMZN call options. Despite not owning any shares of AMZN, the fund is able to simulate the daily price movement of AMZN by utilizing calls and selling put options. The fund implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy and typically selects a strike price that is set between 5% and 15% above the current market price of AMZN. While the upside is still capped at whatever the selected strike price is, this method allows for some capital appreciation to occur.

After owning a plethora of different YieldMax ETFs, the reality is that these large distribution rates are simply unsustainable. Therefore, investors should anticipate that AMZY's share price will eventually erode over time. This is by design, and the only scenario where AMZY's share price will rapidly increase is if AMZN goes on a strong rally for a prolonged period of time. Despite this weakness, I believe that AMZY is still great for investors looking to capitalize on the uncertainty and volatility of the tech market.

AMZN recently reported its Q3 earnings, and the results were strong. AMZN has grown to be one of my largest individual positions, and I believe that the growth is likely to continue over the next decade. In the near term, AWS is likely to be a major contributor to their earnings growth. Over the long term, I believe that AMZN will continue to utilize AI and develop its robotics to expand profit margins over time. However, I think there will be lots of volatility with the companies leading the charge with AI, so AMZY is a great accompanying position to harness this volatility and turn it into income.

Performance Comparison

By now it's understood that AMZY is likely to underperform AMZN over a longer holding period. Just to demonstrate this weakness below, we can see that AMZN's total return sits around 81.5%, while AMZY's total return sits around 78.7%. The downside is that AMZY's share price has deteriorated by nearly 30.5% since its inception, while the large distributions can also create a tax burden for investors.

However, I wanted to specifically cover some alternative funds that investors can choose. While their underlying methods for generating income, holdings, and approaches to harnessing volatility may be different, the basic premise of providing an elevated distribution rate for shareholders remains similar. I believe that the most compelling peers in the high-yield sector are the two funds below:

  • Roundhill AMZN WeeklyPay ETF (AMZW) - dividend yield of ~45.4%.
  • Kurv Yield Premium Strategy Amazon ETF (AMZP) - dividend yield of ~19.2%.

As we can see below, AMZW is the newest addition to the group, with an inception dating back to June of this year. Conversely, AMZY has the longest operating history, largest base assets under management, and highest expense ratio. AMZP is the lowest-yielding choice and issues payouts on a monthly basis, unlike the other two funds that issue weekly distributions. AMZP utilizes the most similar strategy to AMZY, which includes synthetic option writing. AMZW is the most different from the group because the fund doesn't write options. Instead, AMZW utilizes leverage in an effort to capture 120% of the daily price movement of Amazon.

Based on these differences, I believe that each of the funds has a different use case that best suits specific scenarios. With the market's elevated volatility, I believe that AMZY is the best choice at this time. However, here is how I believe each fund should be utilized:

  • Amazon trading choppy but trending upward = AMZY because its higher yield can provide favorable income levels.
  • Amazon is experiencing a strong rally = AMZW because the leverage can amplify the upside and provide attractive income.
  • Amazon flat or declining = AMZP because the lower yield means that the fund is likely to experience the least amount of NAV erosion.

Since AMZW's operating history is so short, the performance comparison is limited. However, we can see that AMZP has outperformed the peer group, despite offering the lowest yield. In fact, AMZY has the lowest total return of the peer group over this short operating history. I believe this is simply due to AMZY's inability to keep its share price stable over time. The large payouts and significant decay in share price are the primary drawbacks with many of the YieldMax funds.

When eliminating AMZW from the comparison, we can see how AMZP still demonstrates the most favorable results. AMZP is able to provide shareholders with some capital appreciation while also outperforming AMZY in total return. Therefore, investors shouldn't confuse AMZY's higher starting yield with its ability to provide the highest total return over time. The key contributor to AMZP's outperformance is the dynamic nature in which the fund writes options. When AMZN's share price is increasing, AMZP writes a lesser amount of options so that it can participate in a greater amount of capital appreciation. Conversely, AMZP may write more options during periods of volatility.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.1067 per share, AMZY's estimated annual distribution rate sits around 37.4%. New payouts are declared every Wednesday, and payouts are now issued every single Friday. Therefore, there's a really good chance that the estimated yield is different by the time you read this. However, payouts weren't also on a weekly basis. This change was implemented about a month ago, as payouts were issued on a monthly basis prior to this.

Despite the shift, we can still see some weakness in the fund's ability to provide consistent payout amounts over time. Since distributions are determined based on factors such as AMZY's success with its options, AMZN's share price movements, and the overall implied volatility of the markets, AMZY isn't a great choice for investors seeking a consistent payout amount every single week. The wildly variable nature of the payouts means that AMZY's distributions can be very low during periods of decline, while they are more generous during periods of market rallies. Looking back on the history below, we can see how payouts in March and April were low since markets were declining. Conversely, payouts increased in May as the markets rebounded off the lows.

At the time of my last coverage, AMZY consisted of paid-out distributions using return of capital. I stated that the payouts can be classified as tax efficient, since return of capital distributions have favorable tax consequences. However, the most recent distributions have not shared this same tax friendliness. For instance, we can see below that the latest payout only contained a 0.16% return of capital, which means that we can assume the rest of the payout was funded by net investment income. Therefore, holding AMZY outside of tax-advantaged accounts will result in a tax burden that investors must account for.

So even though I think that AMZY is a great accompanying fund, I believe that the potential tax burden can be a major drag on the performance. Not only is the share price likely to decline over a long-term period, but now investors need to consider how it will impact their taxes at the end of the year. Additionally, one of the main risks is the fact that payouts are likely to decline eventually alongside the share price. As the share price declines, the payouts have to get smaller over time. There are plenty of examples of this across the YieldMax ETFs.

A good example of this playing out can be demonstrated with the YieldMax COIN Option Income Strategy ETF (CONY). As the share price declined over time, so did the payouts. CONY is a more drastic example of this concept, since the share price eroded so much quicker than what has been experienced with AMZY. So far, however, the risk is still relevant here for AMZY in a scenario where AMZN experiences a prolonged downturn.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on AMZY at this time. However, the caveat is that AMZY makes the most sense for investors that are bullish on the underlying growth of AMZN over time and will utilize the fund as an accompanying position. In a scenario where AMZN experiences a prolonged downturn, AMZY's share price is likely to erode investor capital over time. While there are some compelling peers available to investors, I believe that AMZY remains attractive in the current environment of elevated volatility.

data.title: The IREN Rebound Is Starting
data.description:
Investment Thesis

IREN Limited (IREN) finds itself in a strange blind spot in the market, where the headline about Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) was visible to the naked eye, yet few have any idea what such an announcement truly meant. The bull run since my last coverage played out exceptionally well, delivering roughly a 65% gain in just two months, and now, after a healthy correction, the stock appears to have found a bottom and is setting up for a fresh rebound.

The Power Moat: Why IREN’s 3 GW Portfolio Trumps Its GPUs

Within the ongoing AI cycle, many are fixated on GPUs, having forgotten that power, not semiconductors, is now the new bottleneck. What is taking place in America is a structural bottleneck in interconnection. Data centers are being given several years of delay, state governments are declining major substation projects, while hyperscale companies themselves have begun to constrain growth due to insufficiently available transmission. It’s for these reasons that IREN’s secured 3-gigawatt power source is now so strategic.

Childress itself boasts sole ownership of 750 megawatts of grid-connected capacity. Sweetwater tacks on another 2 gigawatts, truly making it one of, if not the, largest not-so-independent development bases in North America. But then, of course, there’s British Columbia, with its one 160 megawatts of uniquely low-cost air-cooled conversion opportunities. But it’s all about timing in these kinds of projects. IREN’s connection opportunities were secured before the land rush in AI, which in itself gives them multiple years of revenue gains on those stuck in permitting lines.

This power plan is closely integrated with IREN’s philosophy of engineering. Childress Horizon data centers are being developed with water-based cooling densities ranging from two hundred kilowatts per rack, while GB300 requires no more than one 130 kilowatts. It's clear IREN has built not for now or for next generation blackwells, but for next generation blackwells too. Future-proofing is many times just marketing jargon, not in this case, though. If graphics cards change, IREN will not have to break any walls or rework any infrastructures. They will just swap out for the next generation.

Scaling beyond Microsoft is also much simpler than it looks. Horizons five through ten offer an increase in capacity of 450 megawatts at Childress, while in comparison, the size of Sweetwater means its growth plan could itself be a multi-year story. Add in IREN’s liquid-cooling footprint, its near-flawless reliability record, and the fact that it hasn’t missed a single commissioning schedule since going public, and you quickly see why it belongs to a radically small group of operators.

These are the companies that actually deliver real power, real infrastructure, real GPUs, rather than the ones publishing press releases about what they hope to build someday.

Can IREN Compete With Nebius? A 2026 Outlook

IREN starts out with an interesting tale in the AI-infrastructure market, yet what remains to be seen is whether such a tale has what it takes to keep up with the breakneck growth rates being postured by players such as Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS). On paper, IREN’s stats seem impressive with targeted ARR growth to reach $3.4 billion in 2026. It’s not too common to have such monetization rates on a megawatt scale, indicating IREN’s familiarity with high-density computation, which few companies have.

The problem, however, isn't ambition, it’s timing. Childress is a full greenfield project. Even with optimal delivery, energization comes in stages, which do not all fit neatly into 2026. Horizon 1 gets to participate in contributing toward the end of the year, but the full 200-megawatt site extends into 2027 naturally, pushing back not only their GPU start, or ramp, but their revenue ramp in a market in which hyperscalers are competing for megawatts to "power on now."

Nebius’s arena is different in every way. With 2.5 gigawatts sold, multiple geographic deployments in Finland, Serbia, Israel, and the UK, and an aided $7-9 billion ARR growth rate by the end of the year 2026, Nebius offers size, pace, and reach in ways IREN simply has no capacity to match, at least not in 2026. Their reach, location, and sovereignty-compliant topology simply provide Nebius with a natural advantage in 2026.

But can IREN, with its limitations, yet compete? Yes, eventually. But not in 2026, as the gap is too wide. IREN's real moment to shine comes with Childress operating at full energy in 2027, not sooner.

Valuation: Why 2027 Makes IREN Look Strikingly Undervalued

What stands out most when looking at IREN’s valuation is how profoundly the market is still pricing it as if the Microsoft-driven AI cloud ramp were hypothetical instead of contracted. Consensus revenue estimates for June 2027 sit around $2.4 billion, yet the forward P/S multiple collapses to barely above 5x, which is remarkably low for a business shifting toward hyperscale-grade AI cloud economics.

The same pattern appears in forward earnings. Analysts project EPS of $1.24 for 2027, which puts IREN at a 38x forward P/E multiple. While these multiples seem high, they certainly represent an extreme value proposition for a business with multiple years of revenue visibility, project margins over 80%, with its workloads trending toward core AI infrastructure, not Bitcoin cycles. If the $1.9 billion Microsoft ARR begins flowing on schedule, these multiples compress even further, making IREN look far less like a speculative miner and far more like an undervalued, early-stage AI utility.

The Only Kind of Risk That Still Matters

Of course, there is no infrastructure story with zero risk. Microsoft is a dependency risk, and if this tenant slows down, puts things off, or renegotiates, it will hit IREN’s revenue growth. Simultaneously, IREN boasts one of the quickest data center expansion cycles in the entire AI industry, which inherently entails some risk with speed. It’s also worth pointing out that, although Bitcoin mining is no longer such a large part of IREN's bottom-line performance, it’s not yet zero-risk either.

Among these, however, the more one peers into it, the more solid its foundation remains. It’s an irreplaceable power portfolio. It’s an engineering platform, not unique, scalable to multiple locations. And its financial model is geared towards growth, not survival. On-demand, liquid-cooled, high-density capacity demand growth, I think, is outpacing what hyperscale companies projected eighteen months ago, and there’s an increasing disconnect between power supply growth and demand, with IREN being one of the handful of companies with multiple GW capacity in excess to deploy it for monetization in compute.

Takeaway

IREN’s setup is far stronger than the market gives it credit for. With multi-GW power secured, liquid-cooled hyperscale capacity coming online, and Microsoft-backed ARR ramping into 2027, the stock now offers rare asymmetry. After a sharp pullback, the bottom looks in, and the next leg higher is forming.

data.title: Salesforce: High Margins, Lower Multiple - A Rare Opportunity In Enterprise SaaS
data.description:
Salesforce (CRM) is an undervalued cloud-based software company that primarily focuses on assisting businesses in enhancing customer relationship management. The company leverages technology to provide a unified platform, helping businesses to efficiently manage their operations.

CRM is a low-leveraged, highly profitable SaaS business, which operates at substantially above-market margins. However, sentiment around the company remains clouded due to artificial intelligence threats, yet I argue AI-powered solutions may strengthen the company's moat and lay the foundation for multi-year success.

The company's revenue growth moderated, but Salesforce is steadily expanding its top and bottom line. The stock is down by roughly 27% over the past 12 months, which resulted in CRM trading at a forward P/E of approximately 21.50, highlighting about a 10% discount to peers at roughly 24x earnings multiple. I think this may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors to diversify in the company, experiencing solid demand and a strong market position.

Therefore, I rate Salesforce a Strong Buy with $292 price target over the next 12 months, reflecting a 20% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. I think the sell-off appears overdone, and the market may rethink the thesis about the company, resulting in a higher valuation.

FQ2 2026: Double-Beat Quarter

On September 3, the company delivered FQ2 2026 results. This was a solid quarterly report, highlighting a double-digit performance and reflecting a double-digit top- and bottom-line growth. However, the market reacted to the results with a 5% decline over the following trading day.

Salesforce posted $10.24 billion in revenue, suggesting a 10% rise on a year-over-year basis, and $98 million above analysts' estimates. The bottom line arrived at $2.91 in diluted EPS, a 14% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $0.13 above what the market had anticipated. I think this was a solid quarterly performance, yet the negative market reaction indicates investors anticipated greater results.

In the earnings presentation, management highlighted that the combined segment of data cloud and AI achieved more than $1.2 billion in ARR, representing a 120% surge on a year-over-year basis. This supports my thesis that the market concerns tied to artificial intelligence appear overdone, as CRM powers their solutions with AI capabilities, and that may actually help them to strengthen their business and market position. The company has also highlighted that it has closed more than 12,500 deals since the launch of Agentforce, validating my bullish narrative and supporting the thesis that AI-related risks may appear overblown.

In addition, Salesforce achieved $9.7 billion in subscription revenue, highlighting an 11% increase on a year-over-year basis. Furthermore, CRM has $29.4 billion in the current remaining performance obligation, which has risen by 11% on a year-over-year basis, significantly contributing to the bullish narrative and supporting my bullish stance.

Salesforce is running on all cylinders, arguing for excellent execution and a positive outlook. The category of services was the main top-line contributor with $2.5 billion in revenue, highlighting an 8% growth over the past 12 months. The sales segment generated $2.3 billion in revenue, up 8%.

The segment of platform and others experienced the most rapid surge, representing a 16% increase on a year-over-year basis, reaching $2.1 billion in top-line contribution. The integration and analytics rose 12%, highlighting $1.5 billion in revenue, and the marketing and commerce was the slowest growing category, reflecting $1.4 billion in revenue, yet 3% increase over the past 12 months.

I think this indicates that CRM is a well-diversified business, which is another significant argument supporting a bullish narrative. The key top-line contributors are posting roughly double-digit increases, and if the company continues growing revenue at such a rapid pace, I think the market may reprice the SaaS company at a higher premium.

Management underscored that the business has achieved 34.3% in non-GAAP operating margin, which appears to be impressive. The company posted $740 million in operating cash flow and $605 million in free cash flow, supporting my thesis of CRM being a highly profitable business.

Furthermore, the SaaS company continues a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach. Salesforce returned $2.6 billion to its shareholders, through $399 million in dividends, and $2.2 billion in repurchased shares. If such a shareholder-friendly capital allocation strategy is continued, share buybacks could significantly contribute to the company's bottom-line growth.

Management provided guidance for the FQ3 2026 outlook. The company anticipates from $10.24 billion to $10.29 billion in revenue, representing an 8.5% top-line growth at the midpoint, and $2.85 in diluted EPS. If this materializes, a double-digit bottom-line growth would continue further. Furthermore, CRM anticipates approximately a 10% surge in cRPO, which could support bullish continuation further.

Based on the previous quarterly reports and solid momentum in demand, I think the trend is likely to continue. The company remains highly profitable, well-diversified, and running on all cylinders; thus, I think the trend may continue further.

Salesforce is anticipated to deliver FQ3 2026 results on December 3, after the market closes. Wall Street anticipates $10.27 billion in revenue, implying a 9% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $2.86 in diluted EPS, representing approximately a 19% rise in bottom-line performance. I also think that CRM is well-positioned to achieve these results. However, I think investors should monitor whether AI and cloud demand continue, as this would validate that AI-related concerns may appear overly pessimistic.

Premium Business At Undervalued Levels

Salesforce trades at a forward P/E of 21.47, which suggests approximately a 10% discount versus peers at 23.54x earnings multiple. This also indicates roughly 8% undervaluation versus the average company in the S&P500 at a 22.90x earnings multiple. In addition, the chart below represents the current valuation, reflecting a multi-year low for the company, although it continues steady top- and bottom-line performance.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 1.25, CRM trades at a 25% discount versus peers at 1.67, supporting an undervaluation stance and arguing for multiple expansion.

However, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 5.54 and 5.62, respectively, support a more bearish stance for the company, highlighting approximately a 60% premium versus the sector medians of 3.44 and 3.38. This also reveals that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth; thus, although it could continue delivering steady revenue growth, the stock price may not necessarily appreciate in price. However, if revenue accelerates at a mid-10% pace, I argue there is room for multiple expansions.

I would also like to emphasize that CRM remains a low-leveraged business, supporting a bullish narrative. Although Salesforce has $11.81 billion in total obligations, the company has $15.37 billion in cash; therefore, it could pay its debt with ease.

This indicates a few positive tailwinds. It reassures that the company may have financial capabilities to pursue a shareholder-friendly capital allocation strategy through share buybacks, further, which may drive bottom-line growth. Moreover, Salesforce remains well-positioned to operate in both high- and low-interest-rate environments. Over the past 12 months, the company benefited from high-yield accounts, resulting in a positive change in net interest income of $291 million.

Over the past 12 months, CRM grew its revenue by 8.33%, which appears to be roughly in line with the sector median of 8.37%. However, Salesforce trades at about a 10% discount to peers. This creates a disconnect in valuation, and I think the gap may narrow over the following quarters. Moreover, the forward revenue growth rate of 8.89% indicates that the trend is likely to continue, representing a 17% advantage versus peers at 7.57%, supporting my stance on CRM being undervalued.

Furthermore, the bottom line increased by 20% over the past 12 months, and that is significantly above peers and the benchmark. The metric highlights 40% outperformance versus peers at 14%, and roughly 2x outperformance versus the average company in the S&P 500 at about 10% historical EPS growth. This validates disconnection, and this justifies my thoughts that Salesforce deserves a higher premium.

Salesforce operates at substantially above-market margins, and that is another bullish argument for a positive outlook. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 78% indicates approximately a 56% advantage versus peers at 50%. Moreover, the net income margin (TTM) of 17% highlights more than 3.5x outperformance versus peers at 4.67%. These profitability metrics appear to be outstanding, and if the company can continue operating at such levels, it will likely remain a highly profitable business.

To conclude, Salesforce remains a phenomenal business, which is experiencing substantial top- and bottom-line growth, and strong demand, justified by growing cRPO results. Furthermore, it operates at premium margins and trades at a multi-year low valuation, which may argue that the downside appears limited. I think CRM may appear to be a compelling opportunity for investors seeking SaaS exposure with a market-leading stance, excellent execution justified by robust performance across all segments, and an impressive cRPO guiding for a bullish outlook.

Wall Street aims for $11.35 in diluted EPS for fiscal FY2026, and $12.70 in diluted EPS for fiscal FY2027. I agree with such estimates, and I believe strong momentum in demand justifies it. If AI-driven tailwinds continue, the company remains well-positioned to achieve these bottom-line estimates, supported by a shareholder-friendly capital strategy of share buybacks.

Although I think Salesforce deserves a significantly higher valuation, I think the market may reward CRM with a market-like earnings multiple if steady growth continues. I also believe the stock price may mirror bottom-line performance. Therefore, if we apply a 23x earnings multiple to the fiscal FY2027 EPS estimate of $12.70, we arrive at my price target of $292. Time will tell, but my expectations may be conservative if sentiment around the stock improves, and the market rethinks its thesis about the stock. As highlighted above, I think the company is well-positioned to benefit from AI-powered solutions, and I don't think it is causing concerns for the company's business.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Salesforce that could impact the stock price performance in the short term.

Although I think AI-related concerns appear overblown, there is a risk that AI capabilities emerge and may partially overlap with the company's services. However, I am in favor of CRM powering AI enhancements itself, which may result in a sticky customer base and top- and bottom-line growth further.

The company is operating in a landscape that experiences fierce competition from well-known names, such as Microsoft, Adobe, Oracle, and others. If investors think that there is a risk of Salesforce losing market share to competition, the stock could correct and further trade at suppressed valuations.

I also believe that decelerated revenue growth from a high double-digit pace to a moderated high-single-digit expansion may have clouded the positive outlook. Investors may be looking for other alternatives in the SaaS space, which deliver more rapid growth than CRM. However, I think investors should emphasize that Salesforce remains a market-leading company, operating at premium margins, with strength across all categories. It also performs better than its peers, although it trades at a discount.

I think if none of the above risks and concerns materialize, Salesforce may be rewarded with a higher valuation if robust business performance continues.

Conclusion

I think that Salesforce may appear to be an interesting diversification for long-term investors. The company presents a business stability, steady top- and bottom-line growth, low-leveraged capital structure, and phenomenal margins, which are boosting the positive outlook.

The sentiment around the stock remains suppressed, and it could weigh on the company's valuation further. However, I believe AI-related threats appear to be overblown, and with continued cRPO growth, the market may rethink the thesis about the stock. I also think there is a disconnection in valuation, which justifies my CRM's more rapid growth of bottom line than peers, although a discount of earnings multiple. This gap may narrow if growth delivers, yet it may need time.

I rate Salesforce a Strong Buy with a $292 price target over the next 12 months. This represents a 20% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. This price target is mainly supported by expectations of the stock price mirroring bottom-line growth. 53 Wall Street analysts indicate a $332 price target over the next 12 months, presenting a 36% potential. Although I think the SaaS company deserves a higher premium, I favor a more conservative approach, leaving me room for errors.

data.title: AVDV: Capitalizing On Japan's Small Cap Value Surge
data.description:
In this article, I will analyze the Avantis International Small Cap Value ETF (AVDV), an active small cap value ETF focusing on developed markets. AVDV has seen decent traction in 2025, a year where it has outperformed competition and even broader US indices. The AUM has more than doubled in less than a year, chasing this outperformance, as AVDV has surged after years of consolidation.

I am bullish on Japan for the foreseeable future, with a tilt towards small cap and value. AVDV is ideally positioned to capture this expectation. I see AVDV as a decent Buy among peers, and a continuation of the trend set in motion in 2025 so far.

Methodology And Portfolio

AVDV is actively managed. The managers aim to capture the ex-US small cap value space with an emphasis on value characteristics, primarily the adjusted book to price ratio. To measure profitability, measures such as adjusted cash from operations to book value are typically used. The managers also look at relative market cap size within the small cap space for decision making. The market cap weights are the starting point for portfolio weights, over which overweight and underweight biases are added based on value, profitability, and performance signals.

AVDV uses futures and currency forwards to hedge currency exposure as required. There is no fixed rebalancing period defined, which enables agile reactive changes as and when required. Despite the agility allowance, AVDV appears to be reasonably conservative in terms of turnover for an active fund. The 8% turnover in AVDV is slightly over the 2-5% range typically seen in passive international ETFs (VEA has a turnover of 3%, ACWX has a slightly higher turnover of 5%), but it is very conservative compared to active plays (CGIC has a turnover of 43%, JGLO has a turnover of 103%).

The portfolio appears to be well-diversified. There are a little under 1600 holdings, spreading the risk across names. The top 10 holdings account for a reasonable 7.85%, so there is no meaningful concentration risk to be worried about. The portfolio remains top heavy in comparison to its size - it is the size that helps address concentration risks.

Since the emphasis is on small caps, AVDV's sectoral allocations show a slightly higher industrials concentration than usual ex-US developed market ETFs (such as VEA). Financials are weighted lower as it is generally a space where scale is a structural competitive advantage. Consumer cyclicals also get a decent allocation along with basic materials, a segment where AVDV's value tilt naturally focuses on. Tech exposure is sacrificed in favor of industrials and cyclical bets because of the same value focus.

Recent Outperformance Due To Japan

AVDV's returns in the past one year have been ~35% (~42% in terms of total returns), beating even the NDX (up ~22% in the past one year) and outdoing its insipid performance in the past few years before the rally. AVDV has started outperforming very recently, the performance was not close to what NDX offered. Even in the 2020 corrections, AVDV's value tilt did not mean better volatility control. The higher drawdowns come from the small-cap tilt and are perfectly understandable. However, the expected growth is what was missing consistently, even compared to a mega-cap-dominated growth alternative like the NDX - that is where AVDV's overall investment appeal suffered.

Even in comparison with peers, the outperformance is visible only in the past one year. I have compared AVDV's total returns against a peer small cap international value ETF, ISVL. Since the outperformance in comparison with broader US growth indices as well as peers is very recent, the biggest question is whether this breakaway from the peers and broader growth alternatives is sustainable.

To understand whether the outperformance is accidental (aided by positions that did favorably) or sustainable, it is important to understand the differences between AVDV and ISVL. ISVL is passively managed and more rules based than AVDV. This includes higher emphasis on market caps (within the restrictions of the small cap space) and lower agility to take active, forward-looking bets that are possible in AVDV.

Secondly, the forward looking active bets are potentially driven by a systematic theme rather than individual picks going right, given the size of AVDV's portfolio. Even if 10 picks do extraordinarily well, it may not explain the magnitude of outperformance delivered. That systematic theme is the geographical tilt. AVDV tilted heavily towards Japan as Japanese markets rallied in the past year, after years of consolidation. ISVL also excludes Korea, but that is a lesser factor given the dominant weights are in Japan and Europe.

A comparison with ISVL shows the material difference in Japan exposure - a critical reason that explains AVDV's outperformance. AVDV has consistently been overweight on Japan throughout - a theme that has started to play out well now. But, it has used its active management to its advantage by increasing that exposure systematically over time (and before backward looking passive indicators picked up the geographical theme). I picked up the Japan weights in AVDV historically in some date analyses in Seeking Alpha and saw the trend of increasing weights very clearly. In 2021, the exposure to Japan was far lower at ~25%. Even in 2024, it was ~30%, before this outperformance pushed up exposure even higher.

Small cap markets in Japan have been a clear outperformer in comparison to European counterparts in the past 6 months (as shown in the chart below). AVDV's active calls also have a role to play - but Japan small cap markets' overall outperformance remains a key factor aiding AVDV's gains over peers.

Outlook Remains Bullish

Japan is in the middle of a strong equity rally, after years of stagnation. Momentum is on its side. Additionally, corporate governance reforms are underway. Record upticks in buybacks, initiatives to improve RoE and better capital allocation are supporting a revival in equity market interest. And these measures are not restricted to the large cap space. Even small cap names have responded with better shareholder returns. According to a Nomura report, ordinary profit growth for non-financial small caps is expected to be ~11.4% in FY2025 (year ending March 2026). Many Japanese companies trade below book value (~50% as per Franklin Templeton) - a classic value setup.

Putting all the factors together, Japan, small-cap, value are all ingredients of high growth within the developed markets world. I expect the current trend to play out further over the next couple of years at least. Hence, AVDV is a buy. The yields are quite supportive too for investors focused on income. The TTM yield of 3.4% should receive further support from Japan's continued capital allocation favoring shareholder returns. However, investors should view AVDV as primarily a balanced growth play, more than value-income, over the next few quarters.

data.title: Accelleron Executing At A High Level, And Priced Accordingly
data.description:
It’s hard to find fault with the performance of Accelleron (OTCPK:ACLLY) (OTCPK:ACLIF)(ACLN.SW) since this turbocharging specialist was spun out of ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) in 2022. Since then, not only has the company logged impressive revenue growth, margins, and ROIC, the shares have also performed exceptionally well, not only doubling the return of the industrial sector over that time, but also outperforming strong names like ABB, Eaton (ETN), and Schneider (OTCPK:SBGSY), though not keeping up with some names like Wartsila (OTCPK:WRTBY) with similar end-market leverage.

There’s a lot to like about Accelleron. Management has leveraged its market leadership into strong, high-margin revenue (including a robust service business) while continuing to reinvest in R&D and produce substantial free cash flow. With leverage to fuel efficiency, decarbonization, grid stability, and data center capex, I like the growth potential. What I don’t like nearly so much is the extent to which the valuation reflects these positive attributes and drivers, and the case for further rerating is more challenging than I’d like.

Riding Rising Tides Across A Lot Of The Business

Looking back at the company’s midyear update, basically everything is going Accelleron’s way right now. Merchant marine demand has been strong as operators look to improve fuel efficiency and/or adopt alternate fuels, and cruise/ferry demand has been improving. On the energy side, utilities continue to invest in grid stabilization (backup power to complement renewable capacity), and torrid investments into data center capex are driving strong demand for backup power solutions.

Revenue rose almost 19% in the first half of the year, with data center backup power demand driving 25% growth in the High-Speed segment. Gross margin took a hit (down 220bp to 43.6%) as new product growth outpaced high-margin service growth, but operating income still rose 22% (margin up 30bp to 23.3%) and operational EBITA rose 21% (margin up 10bp to 25.5%), with basically balanced growth between High-Speed and Medium/Low-Speed.

As far as bad news goes, there’s not a lot to discuss.

Demand from the gas compression end-market is a little soft on weaker U.S. energy prices, and service revenue hasn’t been keeping pace with product revenue (but given the long lead-times between a new product sale and the start of routine maintenance, that’s not unusual or unexpected). There has also been some modest margin pressure from the business mix.

The biggest issue, though, would appear to be the imposition of a 39% tariff on goods imported into the U.S. from Switzerland. Time will tell if these tariffs stay in place, but they would impact around 10% of Accelleron’s revenue, and they have led management to cut its full-year margin guidance by about 100bp. If there’s a positive here, it’s that the company has already begun efforts to mitigate the tariff, and the company’s position with customers is very strong (and it’s tough to rip out Accelleron turbochargers and replace them with rival offerings).

Data Center Demand Has Supercharged The Near-Term Growth Story

With management projecting 2% to 4% organic revenue growth over the “intermediate term” around the time of the spin-off, the company is clearly outearning its original expectations. A stronger-than-expected marine business is certainly helping, and utility investments into grid stabilization have likewise proven stronger than expected, but data center demand has been the biggest incremental catalyst here.

Data centers consume enormous amounts of power, and the mission-critical nature of these structures demands significant backup power installations. With Accelleron’s turbochargers making generators far more efficient (their turbochargers can increase power output by up to 300%), it’s not too surprising that strong data center capex (leading hyperscalers are on pace to spend more than $100B on data center capex this year) is translating into strong demand for Accelleron, and revenue related to this market more than tripled in the first half of 2025.

To my knowledge, management has never specified how much data center backup power contributes to revenue, but looking at past financial reports and parsing management’s commentary on data center business growth, installed capacity, and so on, this could be a high single-digit percentage (or more) of the business now.

The extent to which this spending binge can continue is a major question now, and not just for Accelleron. There is widespread discussion of whether AI is a bubble and/or is starting to pop, and it remains to be seen if the 20%-plus growth expectations for data center capex in 2026 will hold (likewise the high single-digit to low double-digit growth in 2027).

The eventual deceleration of data center capex spending won’t be devastating to Accelleron’s overall business, but will definitely impact the growth rate and the multiples that the Street is willing to pay. I do expect Accelleron to remain a leader here, but I do think some caution is warranted on this important driver.

The Outlook

I do believe that data center demand is a major driver for the business today, but it’s not as if the rest of the business is bad, nor lacking in drivers. I expect fuel efficiency, greenhouse emissions compliance, and so on to drive healthy marine demand for at least a few more years, and Accelleron’s leading share here will leverage them to grow from customers like Hyundai, Wartsila, and Yanmar.

Moreover, as usage of alternative fuels expands, demand for turbocharging and fuel injection products will outpace overall installed capacity growth, fueling more growth (and likely higher-margin growth) for Accelleron. This will also set up a virtuous circle, as Accelleron will likely capture a meaningful portion of the aftermarket service revenue opportunities with these equipment sales, meaning that “Year 1” sales drive higher-margin recurring revenue starting a few years later.

I’m expecting around 7% long-term revenue growth from Accelleron; while I am concerned that current red-hot data center demand is distorting the picture of what “sustainable” growth looks like, I do expect data center capex to continue growing over time (even if there is a meaningful correction on the way), and I likewise expect healthy demand in the core marine and utility businesses.

I’m assuming high-20%s EBITDA margins going forward, though it is possible that growth in services and recurring revenue could offer some upside. I believe this will translate into mid-teens free cash flow margins over time, driving low double-digit FCF growth, but here too I can see some paths to upside (with 20%+ FCF margins plausible at least from time to time).

Unfortunately, discounting those cash flow streams does not drive a compelling fair value today, even with a discount rate that rewards the company’s strong market share. Likewise, a 20x forward EBITDA multiple, which is not particularly conservative for high-quality industrials (nor data center-exposed names), only gets me to around today’s price.

The Bottom Line

I don’t dismiss the possibility of additional beat-and-raise reports that would lead me to increase my model assumptions. Likewise, the data center capex cycle could still outperform my expectations. All of that said, I feel like expectations here are already pretty robust, particularly given the challenges of the cyclical marine capex cycle.

It’s certainly true that some high-quality industrial names can and do support high multiples that seem out of line from a GARP point of view (names like Atlas Copco (OTCPK:ATLKY) come to mind). It’s also true, though, that these names can be more vulnerable to sentiment and/or weakness in key growth markets (like semiconductor capex for Atlas and perhaps data center capex for Accelleron), and while I do like (and own) this name, I’m more cautious now on valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Adecoagro: Farming Powerhouse With A Crypto Giant Shareholder Taking Big Steps For Expansion
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Adecoagro (AGRO) has been quite volatile recently due to commodity price volatility combined with political and economic uncertainty, with the stock being down ~32% over the past year. Full disclosure from the start: I’ve known AGRO for about 8 years now and bought it for the first time during the crash in 2020, building it into one of the largest positions in my portfolio since then.

They now have 533,000 hectares of land planted, developing significantly over time, currently making money from sugar - which they can sell as is or transform into ethanol based on what’s more profitable at the time - plus various other crops and dairy, being a “pure” farming company with quite green operations, which also allows them to take part in Brazil’s CBio carbon credit system, as I’ll mention below.

Adecoagro’s advantage comes from their flexible and circular low-cost business model that’s quite rare to find, with significant processing and storage facilities across Argentina, Brazil, and Uruguay.

This year’s free cash flow will very likely not be that good this year as a result of the record level of expansion CAPEX YTD that more than doubled compared to 9M’24 thanks to a recent acquisition mentioned below - and that’s on top of going through what they call a challenging price-cost scenario. Note that the company’s cash flow tends to be significantly higher in Q4 due to their seasonality, with the YTD level currently at -$51.2 million (not Adjusted) compared to -$38.29 million in 9M’24.

When asked about the CAPEX for 2026 in response to the current environment, they replied with the following during the Q3 Earnings Release, expecting a significant reduction:

So we are only doing the organic CapEx that really, really makes sense and has a lot of synergies. And so, you can clearly expect a relevant reduction in terms of the growth CapEx coming in each one of our 4 business segments. That's for 2026.

Last year, their Adjusted FCF got to around $56.82 million compared to $108.76 million, a 47.8% drop that’s mostly explained by the higher expansion CAPEX, with 55% higher expansion CAPEX in 2024 ($104 million).

It’s also worth noting that the company still made a ton of money in 2024 despite the significant drop in sugar prices compared to the peak in 2023, with their hedging, flexibility, and other commodities helping to offset this.

Financially, based on AGRO’s latest report, we can see a very solid position, with the current assets of $1.36 billion covering the $486.1 million in current liabilities. As we can see, the company’s equity remained relatively stable at $1.37 billion, which is well above their ~$767.66 million market cap, placing the PB at ~0.56, which is not bad at all even if we’re talking about mostly Brazilian and Argentinian assets. Their debt maturities are also not really an issue, recently placing $500 million worth of 7.5% 2032 notes to repurchase the 6% 2027 notes.

Something very interesting I noticed a long time ago is that Adecoagro’s CEO (also a co-founder) has been refusing the cash and stock-based compensation in the more than 10 years that I checked. In time, we’re talking about millions of dollars that he decided to keep in the company and allow it to develop into what was a billion-dollar company recently.

Dividends & Buybacks

After finishing their 5-year investing plan almost half a decade ago, the company began paying a dividend and doing buybacks, with their policy stating a payout ratio of a minimum of 40% of the adjusted free cash flow from operations, with a $35 million dividend currently and the rest going into buybacks. Currently, that’s a ~4.56% dividend yield plus the buybacks, and we’ll see what they decide during their Q4 release regarding next year’s returns.

Risks & Opportunities

As mentioned before, Brazil’s interest rates were maintained around the same levels - among the highest since the early 2000s. Although the Central Bank expects to maintain the SELIC rate at around 15% and enter into a “very prolonged pause” at this level, these rates will likely be cut once their goals are achieved, continuing the long-term trend of lower and lower rates that can boost the country’s economic activity. Not only that, but it would lower the risk-free rate in the country and potentially lead to more interest in Brazil’s stock market, coming on top of global rate cuts that could change the risk-adjusted yields and opportunity costs globally.

Meanwhile, Argentina is a much more volatile environment, with one of the highest inflation rates on the planet in recent years, sovereign debt issues, and significant reforms following Milei’s victory and the recent win in the country’s midterm, which made the market anticipate a new wave of reforms, and even without them, Adecoagro has been navigating even worse environments for over two decades.

We’ve also seen Milei’s recent moves that can benefit AGRO, like trying to repeal Law 26,737 (“Rural Lands Law”), potentially improving the investment environment, while also lowering the export duties on crops like soy, corn, sunflower, etc. Still, this remains unpredictable and can always change in a couple of years under another president in case Milei loses the elections, and we’ve seen measures/laws hurting them before.

Earlier in 2025, we’ve seen Tether - the group behind the USDT stablecoin - buy another ~49.59% of the company, owning about 74.7% of Adecoagro now after starting their position in September 2024, ramping this stake up significantly. This is part of Tether’s broader move and strategy to invest in real-world assets, infrastructure, agriculture, and energy (recently buying some gold royalty as well), with the deal apparently aligning with their long-term vision of using blockchain and tokenization to build economic resilience, which is something to keep in mind, and I expect them to do something with these real-life commodities, other than the announcement that they’re working on powering Bitcoin mining with renewable energy in Brazil.

Tether has Hadron, a platform built to convert these real-world assets into blockchain-based tokens, and Adecoagro was already a founder and minority owner of Agrotoken, an Argentina-based agricultural commodities tokenization platform with stablecoins like SOYA, CORA, and WHEA. I can’t call myself a fan, but it has the potential to boost Adecoagro’s earnings without doing much, and if it doesn’t work, they don’t really lose much.

However, what this Tether deal also means is that there’s now a risk of being bought out at lower prices. If Tether willfully bought half of the company at over $12 per share, why wouldn’t they want to buy the remaining ~30% stake at $7, for example? What’s interesting is that they bought this new 50% stake at more than $12 per share, and the stock fell even below $9 shortly after it and ~$7.7 now.

At the same time, it can prevent the stock from falling too much, since the market should be well aware that a significant drop could make it way too attractive for Tether to ignore and not try to buy them, although buying such a large stake also limits the stock’s liquidity and can lead to more volatility. Especially with that big stake bought above $12, it’s likely that the remaining shareholders will not want to sell it for significantly less.

A main reason why the stock fell this year was the drop in oil prices. You may be inclined to think that’s good because they need fuel in planting and processing, which is true, but there is a very important aspect to remember. Sugarcane is used to make ethanol (probably the most efficient way to do it), and when oil goes up, ethanol or higher ethanol content gas becomes a more attractive fuel - especially in Brazil, where it’s so predominant. Then, demand rises and the price follows.

When oil prices fall, ethanol eventually has to follow because if it remains high and oil goes down, then you don’t have the same demand. In turn, companies like Adecoagro may decide to produce less ethanol and just make sugar. But then, you have more supply for the same demand, so prices eventually have to fall regardless.

Still, this correlation is not perfect, of course, and we can see delays (sugar production takes much longer, so it’s not as “instant” as oil, being impacted by weather, yields, etc.), and sometimes Adecoagro even follows oil prices more than the relatively less volatile sugar, especially if we look at the performances since the pandemic - partially because oil plays a key role in farming as well.

Still, something seen since Tether took this position is Adecoagro buying a 50% equity stake in Profertil from Nutrien (NTR) for about $600 million (80% AGRO, 20% Asociación de Cooperativas Argentinas), positioning AGRO as a low-cost urea producer in Argentina and reducing their volatility while accelerating their growth with a disciplined financial approach, in line with Tether’s value-creating strategy.

This is probably the boldest and biggest deal in AGRO’s history, expanding their reach into a very important market that has significant potential, as CF Industries (CF) also mentioned in the image above - which I recently upgraded to a Strong Buy in this analysis. As they show, there are potentially significant supply gaps, especially due to the expected European closures due to inefficiencies and policies, so getting this during relative pressure and uncertainty in the market seems like a very solid move from AGRO.

The rising use of biofuels (especially in Brazil) can also be a potential catalyst for AGRO, together with the Make America Healthy Again policies that can lead to less ultra-processed foods, including high-fructose corn syrup and other corn-based products. I talked more about this in the ADM (ADM) analysis posted recently, with corn being a very subsidized crop to the point where it’s present in nearly everything and inefficiently used to make ethanol, with sugarcane being clearly superior. Although I don’t expect the core of US farming to change overnight - if at all - it could be a massive catalyst for a company like Adecoagro, as it would come with significant effects on their key produce.

Of course, risks remain since the company depends on so many commodities (with many of the recent years being quite weak because of economic and environmental conditions), while their exposure to Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay comes with potential political risks. Not only are they exposed to the countries’ internal policies, but they can also be relatively mixed in regard to Trump’s policies, with Adecoagro having operations both in Argentina - who Trump is friendly towards - and Brazil, who has been subject to special tariffs and saw a lot of attention from China as it replaced the US prior to the recent US-China deal that removed the latter’s halt of US soybean imports.

Valuation

To be more conservative, let’s assume $0 in Adjusted FCF in 2025 (will likely be higher in reality) to take into account the higher CAPEX and lower prices, but getting back to $100 million in 2026 (assuming some growth vs. 2024’s level thanks to Profertil and reduced CAPEX, as expected), growing at a 5.5% CAGR until year 5 to take into account their other expansion initiatives, carbon credits, and potential rate cuts, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.75% terminal growth rate. I’ll assume a 9% WACC (above my estimated 8.53% based on a cost of equity of ~11.2% and ~6.8% cost of debt), with $871.1 million in net debt, $64.6 million in non-controlling interests, and 100.09 million shares outstanding based on their Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $1.62 billion, an enterprise value of $681.16 million, and an intrinsic value of $6.81 per share, nearly identical to the current level.

Conclusions

I rate AGRO a hold now, with a value that correctly reflects their potential in the current market conditions, strong fundamentals, and great assets that I’m happy to own. I would say Adecoagro is probably still my favorite investment of all time, and it’s an asset that I want to hold for decades ahead. I like the exposure to vital commodities that they offer, the very profitable operations, and the very nice versatility, especially following the move into urea.

The management is also among the best I’ve seen from many points of view, and they really developed the company a lot following the previous investing cycle, with recent investments on top, and I’m looking forward to adding more during distressed prices.

data.title: Green Debt Market Passes $3 Trillion Milestone
data.description:
By Alan Meng, Research Lead, Sustainable Fixed Income | Lee Clements, Director, Applied SI Research, FTSE Russell

Green bonds show resilience with Europe and Americas down offset by APAC surge

Green fixed income markets entered 2025 under a cloud of uncertainty, with headwinds from macroeconomics, political polarisation and geopolitical tensions[1]. But three quarters into the year, as the dust begins to settle, green bonds have continued to demonstrate resilience.

By the end of Q3, 2025 green bond issuance reached US$467 billion[2] – up 1% from the same period last year and keeping 2024’s full year record of US$572 billion within reach. This has been helped by a healthy overall market for debt issuance, with the share of green bonds relative to total global debt issuances dipping only slightly from 4.5% to 4.3%.[3]

As issuance momentum continued, the total outstanding for green bonds exceeded US$3 trillion at the end of Q3 2025 for the first time.[4] This means the global green bond outstanding universe has expanded at around 30% compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) over the past 5 years – underscoring the accelerating demand for climate finance to support mitigation and resilience, as highlighted in the recent LSEG Investing in the Green Economy 2025 report.

Figure 1 - Green bond issuances show resilience amid volatility

A slump in US corporates and soft issuance in Europe offset by strength in Asia

Europe remained the dominant source of global green bond issuance in 2025, with US$256 billion issued (or 55% of the total volumes), even if year-to-date (YTD) issuance is down 5% year-on-year. In the Americas, issuance dropped even more sharply - down 13% from the same period last year. In the U.S., corporate green bond issuance plunged by nearly 60%, but the municipal segment showed notable resilience, rising 30% and keeping overall U.S. issuance roughly on par with the levels reached during the same period last year. The slowdown across these regions was offset by resurgent issuance in the Asia-Pacific, led by China, where onshore green bond issuance doubled year-on-year.

Green bond markets continue to innovate and diversify

Corporate issuers – both listed and privately held – continue to lead the green bond market. They accounted for about two-thirds of 2025 issuance, with Financials, Utilities and Industrials in the lead. Sovereigns, too, relied on green bonds to mobilise resources in innovative ways. In April, China issued its first-ever sovereign green bond on the London Stock Exchange’s Sustainable Bond Market (SBM).[5] Denmark also launched the first sovereign bond under the new European Green Bond (EuGB) Standard,[6] structured as a ‘green twin bond’. This concept was pioneered by the German government and allows investors to observe price differences between green and conventional bonds.[7]

Use-of-proceeds data also shows that green debt markets continue to diversify. While mitigation themes such as renewable energy, energy efficiency and low-carbon transport still dominate, over a quarter of the eligible use-of-proceeds categories in the overall green bond market relate in some form to adaptation and resilience investments.[8] In the UK, for example, around 12% of its green gilt proceeds support flood and coastal-erosion management.[9]

Sustainable bond funds continue to show steady inflows, with green bond performance aligning closely with conventional bonds

The performance of green bonds – as measured by FTSE World Broad Investment-Grade Green Impact Bond Index (Green WBIG) – continues to closely track its benchmark, the FTSE World Broad Investment-Grade Bond Index (WBIG), despite slight underperformance year to date (see Figure 2). Note that the relative performance of green bond portfolios is generally sensitive to rate increases due to their positive active duration profiles underpinned by a higher concentration of long-duration government green bonds.

Figure 2 - Green bonds performance since 2018 (29 Dec 2017=100)

Sustainable bond fund flows have also continued to show resilience. According to LSEG Lipper data, 46 of the past 60 months have recorded net inflows – a strong signal of sustained demand, broadly mirroring the recovery of the wider fixed-income market following the 2022 sell-off. In March and April 2025, the segment saw brief outflows amid heightened volatility. However, sentiment quickly recovered, with May posting the second-highest monthly inflows of the past five years. Overall, since October 2020, sustainable bond funds have attracted cumulative net inflows of US$54 billion, underscoring enduring investor confidence in green and sustainable fixed-income strategies.

Figure 3 - Sustainable bond fund saw US$54 billion net inflows over 60 months

As 2025 draws to a close, the green bond market continues to demonstrate remarkable resilience - despite prevailing uncertainties and slowing growth in some regions. While short-term issuance and performance may remain influenced by macroeconomic conditions, the underlying fundamentals - rising financing needs for climate-mitigation and adaptation infrastructure[10], strong investor appetite, and stabilising performance in line with the broader fixed-income market - suggest that green bonds will remain a cornerstone of sustainable finance portfolios.

Footnotes

[1] FTSE Russell, LSEG, Sustainable Investment Insights Report 2024 Q4.

[2] Note the issuance figures are sourced from LSEG fixed income databases, which cover different asset classes, including corporate and government bonds, securitisations and US municipal bonds. Specifically, the annual issuance data is derived from Deal Intelligence and US Municipal Bond databases, and outstanding volumes are based on GovCorp database.

[3] Green bond ratio represents the principal amount of green bonds as a share of the overall newly issued bonds in each year, and it does not include US Muni bonds and securitisations.

[4] Cumulative green bond issuance reached approximately US$ 3.5 trillion as of Q3 2025, covering labelled green bonds, green securitisations, and green US municipal bonds, etc. Figures may differ across other market sources due to variations in data coverage, classification and eligibility criteria.

[5] LSEG, Ministry of Finance of the People’s Republic of China launches inaugural Sovereign Green Bonds Issuance.

[6] Danmarks Nationalbank, The Government has issued a 10-year green bond under the EuGB framework.

[7] Finance Agency, the Federal Republic of Germany, Twin Bond Concept - Deutsche Finanzagentur.

[8] LSEG, Green Economy Report - Investing in the green economy 2025

[9] United Kingdom Debt Management Office, UK Green Financing Allocation Report - October 2024.

[10] According to Climate Bonds Initiative, a total of US$17.8 trillion sustainable bonds is to be issued globally by 2035. The $4.4trn Climate Investment Opportunity.
